Title: Mind Loaded (feat. Caroline Polachek, Lorde & Mustafa)
Artist(s): Blood Orange, Caroline Polachek, Lorde, Mustafa

Title: Hooting & Howling
Comment: Covering my bases as (unless an instrumental round is chosen) the theme is sure to involve hooting and/or howling.  Animal theme? Solved! Action verbs? Done and dusted! Maybe you don't like the theme, ergo you don't give two HOOTs and therefore, impressed, vote for this song.  Maybe the theme makes you wonder "HOW did he choose this song?", ergo votes!

Title: Spy In The House Of Love
Comment: Not sure of the theme but going for secret agents/spies and it gives me an excuse to submit this. Loose and funky from the Was brothers/not brothers. The bass slaps hard and Sweet Pea Atkinson fly. Bought my copy from Probe Records in Liverpool.

Title: I Didn't Come Here For Art
Album: I Didn't Come Here For Art
Comment: Here is my shot in the dark, a song that I heard for the first time last week and that I heard for the second, third, fourth and fifth times less than 30 minutes later.

Title: Disappointment
Comment: As I have no idea what the brief is, I thought I’d pick something that ticks as many previous league round criteria as possible. Here’s a recent cover of a song about a breakup by a band with a member who died because drugs that I wish would reform. RIP Dolores. And much thanks to Sarah!

Title: Is That All There Is? - Remastered 1998
Album: The Best Of Miss Peggy Lee
Comment: Blindly submit a song? Is that all there is to a music league?

Title: The Recycle Bin
Comment: My logic here is that ‘things that can’t go in the recycle bin’ covers a world of items, including ‘that album that no-one bought’. (So that’s the 4 of Us taken care of, sorry Sarah). Also this speaks to me as a person has this conversation on a weekly basis. ‘All you have to do is read the top of the bin!’

Title: Medicine for Horses
Comment: Absurdity with hooks. I don't quite know how to parse this band or this album, but since hearing it last year both my wife and I have played it more than a few times, and the loose rules of this round mean that now you can put it in your ears too.... I figure there's a pretty high chance I nailed the mystery theme with this one too!

Title: Every Ounce of Me
Comment: The last league started with the best of 2025, so I thought I’d start this one with a song from the best album of 2026. Granted, at time of submission it’s the only 2026 album I’ve heard, but realistically, what’s going to be better? Jenny is half of the great Let’s Eat Grandma, who did pretty well in a previous league.

Title: Let the Fireflies Fly Away
Album: Dear Mark J. Mulcahy, I Love You
Comment: It has everything to cover the clandestine theme: animals, drugs, food, hitchhiking chickens, and *shhh*, fireflies. The list goes on in this lilting little ditty on Mark Mulcahy's lauded solo album, Dear Mark J. Mulcahy, I love you, his first album after his wife died.  Mulcahy is well known by some around these parts for his band Miracle Legion or even his fictious band Polaris. It's a mission of mine to expose him to people who may not know about his superior songwriting and singing.  Hope you enjoy, my dear 'flies.

Title: Synthesizer
Comment: The secret theme of the round is obviously musical instruments in the title. Or songs from the best ever party album. Or songs that make me very happy. That’s gotta be it. And this fits all of the above categories and is a certified banger.

Title: The Quiet Joys Of Brotherhood
Comment:
Mimi was Joan Baez's sister.  Richard Farina was a talented folksinger and writer who died aged 29 in a motorcycle accident.  His pal Thomas Pynchon dedicated Gravity's Rainbow to him.

This is a song worth knowing.  It's a poem by Richard which he set to the Irish air My Lagan Love. 

Keywords: Irish, married couple (see what I did there), quiet, joys, brotherhood, drone, psychedelic, the ocean, colors, sand, wind, love, trees, horses, thunder, rainbows, flowers, more love, the evils of humankind, more trees, tires, more horses, more flowers, more wind, more love, more love, more love.

Title: I Know You Love Me
Comment: Even if you've never crowned me a Champion of Music League, I know that you love me.

Title: World Go To Hell
Comment: OK, you got me - if Sarah is choosing the theme, we're in 5-dimensional chess territory, I'm giving up on any kind of strategy to simply offer up a dark, smoky gem, dare I say a little bit Eitzelian in its beautiful desolation, from a new quartet of “Australian art-rock royalty” (they say modestly), and with the two Micks (Harvey and Turner), plus Adalita from Magic Dirt and Marty from Art of Fighting, I ain't arguing. Debut album out now, this one feels apt for the times we’re in.

Title: Signals
Comment:
“2026 is the new 2016”
Bollocks, I say!

Still, I suppose this meme COULD be our mysterious theme for the week... and if so, then it's a worthy excuse to revisit the final album by School of Seven Bells (SVIIB for those in the know), featuring some of the last recordings by former Secret Machines guitarist Benjamin Curtis and vocalist Alejandra Deheza.

The duo wrote the album together in the summer of 2012, before the “most tragic, soul shaking tidal wave that life could deliver” — the announcement of Curtis' cancer diagnosis in February 2013 and his tragic death in December of that same year. Deheza later worked with co-producer Justin Meldal-Johnsen to compile the band's previously recorded material for an eventual 2016 release.

Deheza described 'Signals' as “a 100 percent love song to all of the music that woke us up as kids — that woke us up to the fact that music wasn’t other, that it was in our blood.”

Title: Dulling The Horns
Comment: Love this record. Maybe the theme is "learning to let go" with fuzzy guitars?

Title: mi buena suerte.
Comment: Stumbled on this online somewhere. Loved the guitars and the lyrics are helping my Duolingo practice.

Title: Ride Or Die
Comment: ARXX are a duo from Brighton. Their second album Good Boy is excellent and worth checking out. It doesn’t have any songs with the words live or die though, so here’s the title track from their debut.

Title: i'll die anyway.
Comment:
I reach for me, but I’m not there
I always wonder why I’m here
It’s fine it’s ok
I’ll die anyway

Rather to the point, but kinda catchy, eh?

Title: Live And Die
Comment: TBD

Title: Killing Yourself to Live (2009 Remaster)
Comment:
Black Sabbath at their peak going through the paces. I know 70’s rock might not be your jam, but close your eyes and imagine it’s a small up and coming indie rock band from somewhere obscure. Yes, keep your eyes closed and listen. It’s pretty good isn’t it? Raw, immediate, dynamic songwriting. It flows, it packs a punch, but also has a delicate softness to it. It’s good. You can open your eyes now.

PS. Black Sabbath was previously used in S2. Make of that what you will.

Title: When I Die I'll Live Again
Comment:
The title has Die and Live in it and isn’t the Macca song — that has to be worth extra votes. If only I could find a live version as well. 

A simple gospel blues, more a repeated phrase than a full song. I love the lightness & filigree of the guitar playing anchored by the shadow and gravity of the subject matter. Sounds simple but is overflowing with soul. 

Rev. Gary Davis is a relatively recent discovery (within the last 10 years) as I’d always been put off by the name he shared with one of BBC Radio One’s daytime DJs from the ’80s. "Ooh, Gary Davies" infamous for his for suntans, mullets, and playing endless Sonia records.

Title: It's Been Decided That If You Lay Down No-One Will Die
Comment: I've been listening to quite a bit of Loula Yorke over the last year or two - her thing is probably not for everyone here (what is?), but I find her modular synth journeys just the thing when I need something to unwind to - no words, no build, no drop, just gorgeous spirals of sound.

Title: Die Young
Comment: I have only listened to a few SE songs, and was happy to find this track to fit the theme this week. Trying to submit only new selections to the league this round. It is not easy, and I listened to a lot of terrible music in the search this week.

Title: I Wanna Live With You
Comment: I’ve been enjoying Opus Kink’s stew of Bad Seeds, mariachi trumpet, sax, jazz and punk lately. This one comes with more funk than they usually display, but the raucous final 30 seconds captures them in all their glory.

Title: Eat Shit Die
Comment: Losing Rob Hirst has me playing old Oz favorites, and this seemed topical for a handful of reasons: Rob's loss itself, the diapered Orange One caught on camera voiding himself unawares, and generally, finding humor in all the seriousness and dismalness of the day.  Sometimes, dumb fun is what one needs.  Sadly, I don't think we have any children in the group for a drivetime singalong opportunity, but that doesn't mean you can't belt the chorus yourself.

Title: Die Slow
Comment: For anyone caught up in Mother Nature's currently icy grip, enjoy a brief respite with this bit of sun-kissed Southern California indie pop from Smack Champion, fronted by vocalist and songwriter Ray Williams. Listeners can probably cite several touchstones, but I think fans of Jenny Lewis and/or Rilo Kiley will find a kindred spirit at work. No album (yet), although this and several other singles from 2024 await eager ears on Spotify and other streaming platforms.

Title: Born To Die
Album: Born To Die - The Paradise Edition
Comment: This was very hard and I'm not sure how much I  like this song. Hope someone likes it.

Title: We Hate You Please Die
Comment: Surprisingly tough round for me this. plenty of songs but i don’t like many of them enough (or way to obvious). This from french punksters We Hate You Please Die who I saw recently will blow the cobwebs away though. Well done to whoever beat me to Opus Kink btw, first time I've ever been red, or indeed yellow, boxed.

Title: (You Must Fight To Live) On the Planet of the Apes
Comment: Bona fide Mummies are my selection for a "live" or "die" theme, this is 5D chess folks, keep up.

Title: Nobody Can Live Forever
Comment: "Sooner or later, sooner or later you gon' understand that, nobody can live forever." Tim Maia was delivering that hard truth over a funky groove back in 1976. A larger-than-life figure in Brazilian music, he was unapologetically himself: outspoken and open about his cocaine and marijuana use. Known as the father of Brazilian soul, he left a lasting mark on world music before his death in 1998.

Title: Never In Anger
Comment:
Jean Briggs was an American anthropologist who lived with and documented a Canadian Inuit family for 17 months.  The key finding in her 1970 ethnography, Never in Anger: Portrait of an Eskimo Family, was about how the children were taught emotional regulation and resilience through gentleness and playful storytelling (like the Inside Out film itself?), as opposed to yelling or physical punishment.  Apparently Briggs was evicted from the igloo for a while because of her prickly temper, heh.

If I'm honest, I don't even know what the lyrics are trying to say about Briggs or her book (which I haven't read), but the singer's voice and the dynamics of the song work for me.

Title: Joy & Pain
Comment: So this one is going to take a little patience and imagination. It’s a couples only skate at the rink and this smooth track is the perfect soundtrack track for you and your sweetie to glide effortlessly around skating rink. Cocooned in the disco ball glittering lights and this silky tune you float around the moment of Joy with the pain waiting for you to take the skates off later to unveil the enormous blister on your heel.

Title: Fear
Comment:
Satisfies 150% of the brief. The sadness of finding a new song/band, and then seeing the massive streaming count.  How have we not intersected?  The rock I live under is larger and heavier than I thought.  He peaked at 410M with 2013's "Blondie", while this has a substandard 190M streams.  "Do better, young fella", I say disgustedly.  "Don't look back in anger!"

It's the swell at 1:15 that elevates this above simple bedroom lo-fi Cure. https://currentjoys.bandcamp.com/album/a-different-age-2

Title: Sadness As A Gift
Comment: I like her acoustic version of this more than I like this, but this is still worth a listen and it contains the word Fireflies.

Title: Just See Fear
Comment: This was a first - yellow boxed on my first selection. So, here's a track from the gray skies of the rust belt... the sound here is more anger than fear to my ear. But one leads to the other anyway, doesn't it? I wonder how many submissions this week will be emo, or emo adjacent?

Title: Sadness Is a Blessing
Comment: It’s an Eitzel league so it had to be sadness really, didn’t it? A highlight from a fantastic album. The song itself actually brings joy, but the fact that it came out 15 years ago brings sadness, fear, disgust and anger.

Title: Pure Joy
Comment: Indeed

Title: Fear Is Like a Forest
Comment: This duo is not a repeat (CB has been in a round, solo). Now that that is off my chest - I really like the sound of these two together. Jen Cloher, another Australian musician (and Courtney's partner at the time) wrote this song off their 2017 record.

Title: Anger
Comment:
It's March 24, 1977. Just another Thursday for most folks.

But for Brother Marvin, who has been GOING THROUGH SOME THINGS, it's time to testify. Love has not passed away easily, and the scars left behind can not be denied.

Thus begin the sessions for 'Here, My Dear', the infamous double album about the end of his first marriage.

"I did the record out of deep passion. It became a compulsion. I had to free myself of Anna, and I saw this as the way... I knew I’d explode if I didn’t get all that junk out of me. So I had Art open up the mikes. I sang and sang until I drained myself of everything I lived through."

Yet even in his anguish he knew that he could not let himself be consumed, that he would not surrender.

"Oh, reason is beyond control
And the things we do for spite
Makes me ashamed and I mean this, baby
Makes me want to do things right

Someday soon, I hope and pray like Jesus
I'll reach that wiser age
Hope I will learn I really never, never profit
From things I do in rage"

Title: Now There's That Fear Again
Comment:
Michelin-starred-restaurant description: Rustles, Breath, Shimmer, Guitar, Brass, Zippo

Múm always seemed to me to be trying to evoke the dim corners of childhood experience (their first album opened with a song called “I'm 9 Today”), so forgive the elfin voice, please. In this spooky little episode, something is going on with jumping off bridges on bikes, floating downstream, and so on. They pack quite a lot of weirdness into under three minutes, while still delivering some sweet tunes. 

Bonus fact - they once made a song with Kylie Minogue! Her voice fit in pretty well.

Title: Joy
Comment: While playing word roulette to find a suitable submission on the Spotify, I landed on this little gem.  Though I am not familiar with the band, full disclosure, they were submitted previously in first ML. I kindly ask you to relax your itchy DV finger and enjoy the song. This song immediately appeals to that Nico/Velvet Underground fan in you. Plus, dammit, I need this small declaration of building joy right now. We all need it!

Title: Tristesse
Artist(s): Gabriel Fauré, Mac McClure, Marisa Martins
Comment:
A petite piece of French sadness for you from Faure, a composer, organist, choirmaster and head of the Paris Conservatoire. This song was written in 1865 and contrasts the relief and beauty of the onset of spring with a personal sorrow that can’t be shifted: the black dog of depression.

The lyrics scan like a gothic rip in the fabric of time; I can picture a 19th-century Nick Cave sipping absinthe in a Left Bank bar, playing an out-of-tune piano and singing…

"In scanty white dresses,
young girls pass beneath the arbours on their lovers’ arms;
the languishing moon silvers their long, insistent kisses.
Alas! A dreadful sadness afflicts my heart." (In French)

Title: Day of Anger
Comment: I’m a big fan of spaghetti western and their soundtracks, and this is one of the best. If you REALLY like it there are 8 alternative versions on the album.

Title: Fear Of A Black Planet
Comment: More of a Wu Tang and NWA fan, but PE still scratches an itch now and then, and this track fits the theme.

Title: Let's Dance to Joy Division
Album: Proudly Present... A Guide to Love, Loss & Desperation
Comment: To be clear I am not a fan of this band who to my mind have achieved a level of success well beyond their natural footprint but this early offering when they were but nippers makes me smile and hopefully it will do the same for you.

Title: Sadness Soot
Comment: No solo Grant-Lee Phillips in ML yet? Madness! Or Sadness, as is the case here. But instead of another alt folky effort about the futility of everything, this is a song about shaking off the sadness soot! A bit of a sonic departure by GLP, this was a breath of fresh air at the time. Shake off the sadness soot!

Title: 9am (Remastered 2023)
Comment: I was thinking about how different times bring different vibes, and if, say, 5am is the coolest time, because you're obviously staying up VERY late on drugs, what is the most innocent hour? Maybe 9am, when you're probably not still up from the night before (not at our age) but more likely just facing another day of adulting. Is that where Bad Bad Hats are coming from? Not sure. I think maybe they HAVE been up all night, but you decide.

Title: Midnight Zone
Comment: I just discovered them, but I think this is what the cool kids were listening to in 2017.

Title: Midnight City
Comment:
"The city is my church
It wraps me in its blinding twilight"

The hypnotic first single from M83's 'Hurry Up, We're Dreaming', just as glorious almost 15 years later... perhaps risking the familiarity tax for what was later described as "an unavoidable Millennial classic" but I still love it. The closing saxophone solo (played by James King from Fitz and the Tantrums) takes you to that final destination, wherever it may be.

You might also remember the accompanying conceptual music video, which shares little DNA with the song itself but still contains its own dark pleasures.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dX3k_QDnzHE

Title: 5 AM
Comment: By the time this track dropped my clubbing days had disappeared so far in the rearview mirror to be almost invisible. The sentiment still resonated deeply like a bass drum kick from an 808. Sweet memories of serotonin soaked sunrises. Dazed and confused watching the dawn break. Sometimes it was sat in hilltop parks,  sometimes in bus shelters, sometimes on the  top deck of a rowdy night bus. Always smiling, often laughing and in need of a cold diet coke, a lay down and to chill out to Eno's Apollo: Atmospheres and Soundtracks.

Title: 5 a.m.
Comment:
Music League forces you to stretch a bit both in selecting a song and then again in the listening to the (sometimes unhinged) selections of the other maniacs you play with. It's been by far the best way to discover actually new-to-me music.

I usually try to start with songs I know well, because it's easier to provide a write-up, but it's become much harder to do that while still finding a fresh artist after so many rounds. This one took a bit of browsing to discover, but what a find....  Roughly two and a half minutes of sunshine pop, but with lots of layers. The percussion in particular makes this an interesting listen as it starts off simple and then adds a subdued but busy layer of bongos.

If the internet is to be believed, apparently this (apparently much beloved) album was the most expensive recorded to date at the time in 1968. Enjoy!

Title: Gimme! Gimme! Gimme! (A Man After Midnight)
Comment: Does the familiarity tax have to be paid when we are talking about an all time classic? About a song that has reached the culturaI heights of GGGAMAM?  Even when one interpretation of the lyrics might be - "I'm a bit sad now, but if I get some cock later I'll be grand?" (Thanks ChatGPT).  I would submit not.  This song is a work of art.

Title: Six O'Clock News
Comment: It was either this or the John Prine song by the same name. This one is 24 years old--just revisited this record after enjoying her 2025 release. She played this one on Letterman in 2002. Her music has always reminded me of a less folksy Mary-Chapin Carpenter.

Title: 7 heures du matin
Comment:
Which version to submit? The original in French has more teeth. Honestly, nothing slaps quite like 60s French pop—it’s pure, unadulterated groove. Enter Jacqueline Taïeb, the Tunisian-born queen of the late 60s Yé-yé scene.
​While most of her peers were basically puppets for male songwriters, Jacqueline was the ultimate DIY rebel, writing and composing her own tunes. She wasn't just a face, she was the brain behind the beat.
​
​In 1967, she dropped "7 heures du matin," a garage-rock masterpiece about Monday malaise.
It’s a fuzzy, frantic internal monologue of a teenage girl who absolutely cannot deal with a Monday morning. Backed by a gritty "fuzz guitar" to ground her chic Parisian cool. 
She name-drops her idols—The Who, Elvis, and Paul McCartney—while procrastinating on her homework.​ It's the perfect anthem for anyone who has ever stared at their ceiling instead of getting ready for work.

Title: Seven Minutes to Midnight
Album: Nah = Poo - The Art of Bluff
Comment: Seven minutes to midnight, 11:53 (or 23:53) is pretty specific time right? This is one of my favourite songs of all time. Just saying.

Title: 5 O'Clock In The Morning
Comment: It’s 1977, punk is on the rise, you’ve  recently left 10cc. Of course you release a triple concept album about “the story of man's last defence against an irate nature”, featuring a play with Peter Cook playing multiple characters and highlighting your “Gizmotron” device to provide orchestral textures from your electric guitars. Critical reaction at the time has been described as “hostile”.

Title: November, 4AM
Album: Bringing Home The Last Great Strike
Comment:
While Richmond Fontaine brings Raymond Carver to mind, Pinetop Seven calls up Zane Grey or Louis L'Amour, and their tales of miners, gamblers, and cowboys in various stages of luck and life.  Peep the gunfighting cover of their 1997 debut.

Darren Richard's voice accentuates the western lyrical scenery, warm and road-weary.  This song, their first in the league, finds the man early morning in bed with his love, feeling unworthy of her.  

I can relate to the lyric, "Now my knees are an older man's / I'm scared of what they hide".

Title: 25 or 6 to 4 - 2002 Remaster
Album: The Very Best of Chicago: Only the Beginning
Comment: A very good song written about the struggle of writing an entirely different song. “25 or 6 to 4” references the time on the clock songwriter Robert Lamm saw while trying to rewrite the lyrics of another song. A killer riff, prime Peter Cetera, propulsive horns, this song was made for this round.

Title: Midnight at the Oasis
Comment: Testing

Title: High Noon Amsterdam
Comment: Noon is a specific time. And it sounds as if Chris Goss is both enjoying and loathing himself in the harsh light om day in this bit of swinging stoner rock.

Title: 4am
Comment: What time is it? Time for a cheery ode to inescapable loneliness from some Mancunian romantics. When is it not?

Title: Wednesday Morning, 3 A.M.
Comment:
If you don’t hear from me, I robbed a liquor store shortly before the list released and got nabbed for being a ham-fisted fuckface. It’s been cool.

Peace Love & Anarchy

Title: Chains of Love
Comment: Of all the ways I could have  gone with this theme I assumed I'd play it safe and end up in the 60s or 70s. In a way that's what happened, just you know... filtered through some early 2000s fuzzed-up grit.

Title: Out Of The Sky
Comment:
“Out of the Sky” first landed in my life thanks to an ex who slipped it onto a mix CD for me back in the early 2000s. You know how some songs just sneak up and stay? This was one of those.
It opens with a bit of loose, conversational chatter that instantly pulled me in, kind of like the spoken intro vibe on Marvin Gaye's What's Going On. When the band eases in suddenly you’re surrounded by all these delicious layers: a funky guitar line, a bass that refuses to sit still, and Van’s honey-smoked voice gliding over it all.
Lyrically, he’s tracing that arc we all know too well: the thrill of the climb and the hard reality of the fall, tumbling “out of the sky.” It’s soulful and thoughtful without ever getting heavy-handed. By the time those playful “doo doo doo doo” harmonies show up, punctuated by tight little horn bursts and that sweet a cappella finish, the groove has fully claimed you.

Title: Best Be Believing
Comment: I know at least one other player is a fan of this album so thought I might get yellow boxed. Could have chosen any number of classics but this 2013 album is one I return to frequently, a debut that sounds like a greatest hits with not one track that couldn’t be a single. They are very poppy, but Wikipedia says R&B and who am I to argue.

Title: Sara Smile
Album: Daryl Hall & John Oates (The Silver Album)
Comment:
Feels like I'm pushing all my chips in and courting danger in the voting.

But hear me out... if ABBA and Chicago can land toward the top of the table, so can Hall & Oates. They can't all be deep cuts and lost gems. The bigger problem here was choosing a song from the pile of contenders to showcase that famous buttery vocal range on DH.

Not sure there is a single correct choice, but I am a gentleman, and if I'm gonna play you something you've already heard a thousand times, at least I can choose a short one so you can quickly get back to the obscurity.

Deep down though, you know this is the one that's gonna get the people singing along. (To be clear though, they should not: nobody should attempt to keep up with Daryl Hall.)

US based players should also add the "Callin' Oates" hotline (719-26-OATES) to your contacts if you haven't already done so, for emergencies.

Felt bold, might delete later.

Title: Stormy Weather
Comment: This round is not my home turf, but Etta James' voice is no doubt, the bomb. The song has been recorded in all kinds of ways over the years, and I fretted a bit about whether Etta's version is "soul/R&B" enough, but to me her vocal seals the deal. There's something wonderful about the simplicity of that album cover too.

Title: The Snake
Comment: Not really my genre but always loved this northern soul classic.

Title: Return of the Mack
Comment: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Return_of_the_Mack  “a song written and recorded by British R&B singer Mark Morrison” calms my “is this pop?” worry.  The “you lied to me” delivery, the Tom Tom Club sample, the chorus that never wears out its welcome all combine for a perfect song.  Sorry, Curtis, Al, Sade, and Marvin.  Tough choice to make.

Title: Bring It on Home to Me - Live at the Harlem Square Club, Miami, FL - January 1963
Album: One Night Stand - Sam Cooke Live At The Harlem Square Club, 1963
Comment:
"Everybody's with me! Everybody's with me tonight!"

By the early '60s, Sam Cooke had already achieved massive commercial success by pairing his gospel roots with secular pop music, resulting in dozens of hit songs and earning the title of "The Man Who Invented Soul" (also the title of his excellent 2000 box set retrospective).

But that success was not without tradeoffs. Hard as it is to believe in hindsight, the tapes from this raucous 1963 performance at Miami's Harlem Supper Club were deemed too harsh for Cooke's pop audience and subsequently buried in the RCA vaults for over 20 years, passed over in favor of the much tamer 'Sam Cooke at the Copa' in 1964.

Here, "Bring It On Home To Me" hits with the force of revelation. The performance was later lovingly recreated on film by actor David Elliott as part of the introductory sequence for Michael Mann's 2001 'Ali' biopic.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pAx02ml3Tjo

Title: Pouring Water on a Drowning Man
Album: You Got My Mind Messed Up
Comment: Sweet southern soul from the pipes of James Carr. I first discovered Carr pre internet and streaming via one of those Rough Guides. A little pocket sized one for Soul Music  that had 100 artists and records in it. A gateway to a wealth of amazing music. This is off the first CD (a best of) that I purchased off the back of the book. Loved this track from first listen.

Title: Untitled (How Does It Feel)
Comment: Not sure how well D’Angelo will be known to this crew. I was introduced to him by one of my musician friends who always pointed me in the right direction. The space on this track is immense. The rhythm is a structure the song is woven through with incredible delicacy. He passed away last fall from pancreatic cancer after only 3 albums. Strongly recommend giving Voodoo a listen if you have any interest in R&B or neosoul.

Title: Stay High
Comment: A warm, soulful burst of joy from the singer of Alabama Shakes that's underpinned by what sounds to me like a Fisher Price piano.

Title: Love On The Brain
Comment:
“I'm tired of being played like a violin/What do I gotta do to get in your motherfuckin' heart?”

With a smorgasbord of choices this round, I surprised myself by picking Rihanna, a queen of the digital music era. After a barrage of pop-R&B-dance hits and collaborations, I thought I knew what to expect from her. Alas!

A modern twist on doo-wop soul ballads, this sleeper hit was described as more ‘muted’,  ‘soulful’, and less ‘radio-friendly’ than her past work, showing true artistic maturity. Pitchfork praised ‘newfound introspection and vulnerability’, Rolling Stone applauded she is ‘in charge of her own sound, remaking pop on her own terms’ and similarly, Spin remarked ‘for once, she is drawing on whims rather than characters, uncertainties rather than pronouncements, her own desires rather than the desires of her audience." Motherfuckin’ good for her.

Title: Cry Baby Cry
Comment:
Ramsey Lewis’s 1968 album "Mother Nature's Son" covers 10 songs from the Beatles' White Album.  Some are definitely jazzy, others more soul-powered (it hit both the jazz and soul Billboard charts).  "Cry Baby Cry" is more soul, with Ray Charles-style electric keys, but also contains other magical surprises (dig the spooky/sci-fi Moog intro).

Sorry it cuts off at the end.  On the album, it segues right into the next song, accordingly for that era of experimentation in the studio.

Fun side quest: How much of Fiona Apple's and Aimee Mann's careers derive directly from this groove?  A metric fuck-ton, that's how much.

Title: Every Ghetto, Every City
Comment: This is one of my top 10 records of all time--part r&b, part hip-hop, part reggae. Love her voice, love the image of South Orange, New Jersey she creates here -- makes me nostalgic for a place I've never been.

Title: 7 Years
Comment: So this was kinda a big hit a decade or so ago. It leans pop, but the band clearly has its roots in soul and R&B.

Title: Say My Name
Comment: I am not an expert in either genre, and the classic soul stuff that occasionally gets submitted here does very little for me. I’d be much happier if there was a bit more of this kind of thing. Yes, it has over a billion streams, which some would say might make it over-familiar. It’s slightly more obscure than last week’s ABBA song though (1.10 billion vs 1.13 billion). And it’s really really good.

Title: Panama - 2015 Remaster
Comment: I kept trying to find something "better"but this is my favorite guitar solo. Apparently Eddy Van Halen also recorded his 1972 Lamborghini's engine for this song, too. Song may be a bit cheesy, but not Eddy's guitar.

Title: Limelight
Comment:
https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=ZiRuj2_czzw My definition of a power trio, each a master of their instrument.  And such babies here. Could discuss Geddy’s bass or Neil’s precision, complexity, and lyrics, but this is the guitar solo round.

Alex hits a guitar trifecta on this second ML appearance. He opens with a catalog topping riff, adds color splashes through the verses, then after the 2:30 mark, starts his solo slow and mournful like a Bradbury foghorn and ends with speed and a whammy bar.

For bonus credit, listen to La Villa Strangiato next.

Title: Is This The Life?
Album: A Little Man and a House and the Whole World Window
Comment: My favourite Bowie song is the Sweet Thing/Candidate/Reprise suite from Diamond Dogs. It triggers the whole range of human emotions musically and lyrically. Its opening line is “it's safe in the city - to love in a doorway - to wrangle some screams from the dawn”. And that’s in a way how I like my guitar solos. Not fast fingered fret wankery, not show off technique, all style over content, rather wrangling those screams, a primitive howl, a pure expression of emotion, player and instrument as one. This guitar “solo” does that for me. The whole of the second half of this song is purity, power, passion. Enjoy. x

Title: Shesmovedon
Comment:
These days, Steven Wilson is recognised as the premier mixer and reissue producer for dozens of famous recording artists in addition to his own successful solo career.

But for nearly 20 years he was best known as the leader of British neo-progressive band Porcupine Tree. Heavily influenced by Pink Floyd and David Gilmour, Wilson gained a reputation as a skilled and tasteful guitarist, with typically melodic fan-favourite solos on songs like "Waiting" and "Dark Matter".

"Shesmovedon" reveals a different approach. Here, Wilson punctuates the bitter end of a relationship as detailed in the lyrics with aggression, choosing a decidedly pinched and distorted tone as he doubles the main riff after the first chorus. Following a brief respite, Wilson begins his solo after the final chorus, his playing increasingly wild and frenetic as he channels waves upon waves of emotional destruction and the psychic wreckage left in their wake.

In the end are we left with catharsis, or with capitulation?

Title: Afrique Victime
Comment:
If you've ever dreamt of being caught up in the frenzied, joyous dancing of a desert-dwelling nomadic tribe's wedding party-- and really, haven't we all-- go to a Mdou Moctar concert.  You will be surrounded by many pasty white indie dudes, but it will still be a lot of fun.
Alternately called the Jimi Hendrix or Eddie Van Halen of the Sahara, Mdou shreds the tent to smithereens.

Title: The Asphalt World (Remastered)
Comment:
I considered all sorts for this but I really wanted a solo by Bernard Butler, most famous of course for producing Mark Eitzel but he also did some other bits and pieces. One was the epic bit of drama that forms the middle of this most epic and dramatic of songs. The moment at 6:18 where the solo explodes is pure goosebumps and has always been a highlight of live shows (even if played by Richard Oakes these days). The solo actually seems to do as much storytelling as the lyrics, and the build to the vocals coming back in is nothing short of . I saw Bernard play this live a few times, when Brett would sulkily go off to let him do his thing, returning in the most insouciant last-second way to make it to the mic at the exact right moment. 

PS: yes we have had them before the ‘favourite’ overrules this. 

PPS yes it is 9 and a half minutes but on the other hand I could have gone with the unedited 11 and half minute version.

Title: I Believe in a Thing Called Love - Single Version
Album: I Believe in a Thing Called Love
Comment: 300 plectrums were broken in the making of this glorious slice of swaggering glam rock where the solos are technically skilled yet full of an endearing inability to take themselves seriously. And, Justin says "guitar" as a countdown to the solo which surely exempts it from any downvotes.

Title: Electric Counterpoint: III. Fast
Comment: So this is not a “guitar solo” in the vein of the egotistical self pleasuring most probably visualize by that term. But it is Pat Metheny, guitar virtuoso, expertly weaving the various melodic lines and figures of Steve Reich’s composition into a cohesive whole on his guitar. The 13 different parts move in and out of focus, each of them being the solo and backing at the same time.

Title: Late for the Sky - Remastered
Comment: I've wanted to toss Jackson into the Music League ring for ages, but who would've gave guessed that when the time would come it would actually owe more to his longtime collaborator and guitarist extraordinaire, David Lindley, than JB himself. Lindley's solo is a thing of absolute wonder & majesty, slowly taking us down the winding, mournful corridors laid bare in the verses and chorus, but Lindley's tasteful playing throughout this gem is the undisputed anchor of the compositions musical soul, right up to the last seconds as the guitar notes drift off into the oft-used fade-out of the 1970s.

Title: Carry the Zero
Comment: Big time love for this song. Over the years I return to this album and play it loud in my car when I want to release anger and anxiety. I need the energy it gives. The math in the lyrics and in the music soothe my neuro divergent brain. Doug Martsch's guitar work rightly rocks, but it also has sweet tinges of melody. It is a bit unorthodox to get a solo near the beginning of a song AND at the end of a song but BTS is never conventional in the way they attack music. Ah, Idaho, I love your delicious potatoes and  your Built to Spill.

Title: Space to Bakersfield
Comment:
My dad played rock & blues guitar for 60+ years. So I picked one he suggested, but one with atmosphere that cushions any noodling within and doesn’t hit like a frying pan to the face. This 2016 song comes from a familiar but alternate universe:

“By the late 1970s, many dinosaur rock acts had gone disco/slickened up their sound…through that process, synth would come to represent a musical cancer to purists, the agent through which the majesty of rock got diluted into nancy-boy new wave and poofed-up hair-metal power ballads. [This] reminds us that technologically curious early adopters like Rush, Led Zeppelin, & Pink Floyd were among the original synth-pop bands, using push-button machinery to expand and enhance their fantastical vision rather than cloud it…It presents an alternate '80s where the prog, guitar-powered rock that dominated the preceding decade successfully adapted to pop's synth-centric shift, instead of becoming an instant anachronism.” -Pitchfork

Title: Everybody Wants To Rule The World
Album: Songs From The Big Chair (Super Deluxe Edition)

Title: Let's Call It Love
Comment: Do I submit a 3 minute song with a nice concise tuneful solo, or do I submit an 11 minute song where the last 5 and a half minutes is glorious guitar chaos? Decisions decisions.

Title: Beck's Bolero - Instrumental
Comment: Does this count as a solo? The whole song is a guitar masterpiece. Beck replaced Clapton in the Yardbirds on recommendation from Jimmy Page. Pink Floyd wanted him to replace Syd but were too intimidated by him as a musician to ask. This track is from 1966 and Beck is accompanied here by Page, John Paul Jones, and Keith Moon.

Title: Leg
Comment:
In 1993, a friend visited me at school with an armload of music to share. Among the pile of CD's was this one from this curiously named Dutch band. Turns out that the particular batch of music he arrived with from these indie giant labels became pretty seminal to my listening over the following years.

There are at least a few tracks on this album where guitarist Peter Visser completely lights up his gear. His style owes a lot to Neil Young, but more twisty and dynamic. Somehow he's able to fit a ton of swirling, screaming, fuzzy texture and feedback neatly into the songs by starting with a melodic complement to the vocals before unraveling into controlled chaos as the songs spin out.

There's a small solo midway through here where you might expect it, but then the guitar takes a step back and continues to snake around the vocals before exploding all over the last couple minutes of the song.

The whole album is worth a listen.

Title: Freak Scene
Comment:
I can never pick absolute favourites, and it seems I don't remember guitar solos all that well, but suddenly a name came to mind, one as yet unseen in the Lore of the League, nor yellow/red-boxed on selection. Whatever you call Dinosaur Jr - alternative, early grunge, hardcore-ish - these are not really genres known for guitar solos, maybe even anti-solo, but J is a man with his own vision and a large collection of FX pedals.

What I love about Freak Scene is how it weaves between reasonably clean guitar tones and stomps of noise - check the gorgeous chunka-chunka after the first mildly-fuzzed-out verse, followed by a smooth, almost-folky verse - until the solo cuts loose, perched right on the edge between melody and annihilating distortion, followed by the heartfelt verse with the thrill you/kill you/will you/still you line endings, and a kind of solo reprise to see us out. It's pretty damn perfect.

Title: Marquee Moon
Comment: You want a guitar solo? Well I have two for you. Although it is the second one I am submitting for your consideration and ire. Tom Verlaine starts his solo around 4:50 mark and it carries on until the song coda as it closes. The solo starts at a slow pace, plenty of space between the notes (Eddie Van Halen is this not), before it begins to accelerate, the notes getting closer together, more tension, more treble, higher in register. Is a solo that last for half the song really a solo? I will leave that for you to decide. I love it.
